title: activity — field-level data classification
description: Every column on Activity + ActivityRetentionPolicy classified by sensitivity tier. Drives DSAR export scope, right-to-erasure behaviour, encryption-at-rest policy. Field paths: `Activity.field` / `ActivityRetentionPolicy.field`.

tiers.public: Non-identifying, safe to expose in logs / URLs.
tiers.internal: Platform-internal state. Never surfaced to end users outside admin. Not personally identifiable.
tiers.confidential: PII / user-activity data — exportable on DSAR, erasable on request. Encrypted in transit + AES-256 at rest.
tiers.audit_log: Immutable audit-tier data. Anonymised, not deleted, on erasure requests. Applies to retention policy fields only — Activity rows themselves are HARD-DELETED on erasure.
tiers.restricted: Not present on this model — no credential-adjacent secrets stored here.

confidential: Activity.subject_id (points at a User or an entity that carries PII — e.g. Athlete)
Activity.causer_id (points at a User — actor identity is PII)
Activity.description (may contain user-visible name references — the developer authors these; the consumer contract enforces user-visible narrative not raw payloads)
Activity.properties (structured context — facility names, booking references, previous values; developer-authored, but may carry indirect PII via referenced entity names)
ActivityRetentionPolicy.override_reason (free-text may include tenant admin's name or PII references; retained on audit stream)
audit_log: ActivityRetentionPolicy.override_reason (retained indefinitely on the audit stream via HasAudit — anonymised on subject erasure but never deleted)
ActivityRetentionPolicy rows themselves (the audit rows written by owen-it/laravel-auditing on retention policy mutations — retained 7 years per audit module)
regulated_minor: Activity.description AND Activity.properties WHERE subject_type = 'athlete' AND subject is under 13 (inherits regulated_minor from the athlete). The consumer contract prohibits capturing this data in the first place — LogsActivity should not be composed on Athlete for progress fields; use audit instead.
regulated_health: Activity.description AND Activity.properties WHERE subject_type is a healthcare-adjacent aggregate (medical-records module, future). Prohibited by the consumer contract — those aggregates use audit.

exportExclusions: Activity rows where subject is a DIFFERENT tenant's User (the caller's DSAR reveals only activities on THIS caller's identity — activities on their booking that another tenant's assistant caused are only visible if the caller is the causer)

erasureBehaviour.confidential: purge — Activity rows are hard-deleted on identity erasure. Unlike audit (which anonymises), activity is user-facing narrative; preserving after erasure would leak the erased identity's actions on other users' feeds.
erasureBehaviour.audit_log: anonymise-in-place — ActivityRetentionPolicy audit rows preserve the override_reason (retention promise) but subject_id references get replaced with 'erased-<hash>'. Rows themselves never deleted.
erasureBehaviour.regulated_minor: PROHIBITED at capture time — the consumer contract prevents LogsActivity on minor-adjacent aggregates. Any row that slipped through is treated as `confidential` erasure (hard-delete).

notes: Activity is a CROSS-CUTTING aggregate — it doesn't own the underlying subject or causer PII. It owns the NARRATIVE about them (who did what to whom). The confidential tier reflects that: knowing 'Alice cancelled Bob's booking on Tuesday' is itself sensitive because it reveals interpersonal + operational context.

The `description` column is the tightest classification decision in this file. It's developer-authored narrative — the consumer contract says 'user-readable, no raw payloads', but the developer's actual authored string may include user names via `{$this->causer->display_name}` interpolation. Confidential tier is correct.

The `properties` column is developer-authored structured context. The consumer contract says 'log ONLY minimum fields'; violations leak PII. Reviewed by the standards-steward auditor agent for `properties` shapes containing raw payloads (email addresses, phone numbers, address fields).

Downstream consumer modules with regulated resources (medical, financial) MUST NOT compose LogsActivity. Enforced by code review + the standards-steward auditor. Any violation would create a regulated-tier activity row and require the retention pipeline to be aware of it — activity is not equipped for that.
ActivityRetentionPolicy is small-surface + admin-only. Its confidential tier is minimal (a few user-attribution FKs + the override_reason free-text).
